items.q: What is different between two isotopes of the same element?
items.correct: They have the same number of protons but different numbers of neutrons.
items.misconception: Isotopes have different numbers of electrons, which is why their mass differs.
items.random_wrong: Isotopes are atoms of different elements with the same mass.
items.why: Students conflate isotopes with ions because both alter a property of the atom — but ions change electrons (charge), isotopes change neutrons (mass).

items.q: Does a catalyst affect the position of equilibrium?
items.correct: No — it speeds up both the forward and reverse reactions equally, so equilibrium is reached faster but at the same position.
items.misconception: A catalyst shifts the equilibrium towards the products.
items.random_wrong: A catalyst increases the yield by being consumed in the forward reaction only.
items.why: Students associate 'catalyst' with 'helps the reaction' and assume that means 'pushes towards products' rather than 'speeds up both directions equally'.

items.q: When water boils, what is broken?
items.correct: Intermolecular forces (hydrogen bonds) between water molecules are broken — the O-H covalent bonds stay intact.
items.misconception: The covalent bonds between hydrogen and oxygen atoms are broken when water boils.
items.random_wrong: Water molecules split into hydrogen and oxygen gas when they boil.
items.why: Students don't distinguish between bonds within a molecule and forces between molecules, so they assume boiling 'breaks the molecule apart'.

items.q: Sodium has atomic number 11. Its electron configuration is:
items.why: It's 2,8,1. “2,9” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: An atom of Mg loses 2 electrons to form Mg²⁺. Its number of protons is:
items.why: Correct answer: 12. “10” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: How many neutrons in an atom with mass number 23 and atomic number 11?
items.why: Exact answer: 12 neutrons. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
